I’m 16, and my family is planning a small gathering for my cousin’s 21st birthday. I’ve been asked to help organize the food and seating, but I’m not sure how to handle the elders. My uncle, who’s 68, will be there, and my younger cousin, only 10, is also coming. How should I arrange things so everyone feels respected?

Ah, that’s a thoughtful responsibility you’ve been given—handling arrangements at a family gathering shows you’re being trusted with adult duties, even if you’re still young. In Nigerian culture, elders are always seated in positions of honor. You should place your uncle, your 68-year-old uncle, at the head of the seating area—on the right side of the main table, if it’s a formal setup. That’s the traditional spot for the eldest male, even if he’s not the host. Make sure he’s not seated too close to the back or side; that would be disrespectful.
What about my 10-year-old cousin? Should I let her sit with the elders too, or should she be with the other children?

Good question. Your 10-year-old cousin should sit with the younger children—preferably at a separate table or in a designated area near the elders, but not at the main table. She’s still in the childhood phase, where quiet respect and obedience are expected. She may be allowed to come to the elders’ table to serve drinks or fetch items, but she must not speak unless spoken to. That’s not about being excluded—it’s about teaching her the rhythm of family respect. You can gently remind her: ‘Aunty will speak when she’s ready, my dear.’
I’ve never organized anything like this before. What if I make a mistake—like seating someone incorrectly?

It’s okay to be nervous—this is your first time leading something like this, and that’s part of growing up. The elders will notice your effort, not every small detail. If you do seat someone wrongly, don’t panic. Just quietly reposition them when no one’s looking, or ask your mother or aunt to help. But always do it with humility. You can say, ‘Aunty, I meant to seat you here—forgive my mistake.’ That kind of deference is more valued than perfection. Remember: showing respect through action, not just knowledge, is what earns trust.
And what about me? I’m 16. Should I sit with the elders too, or with the young adults?

That’s a very important question—and one that shows you’re thinking deeply about your role. At 16, you’re still considered a young adult in many Nigerian families, especially in rural or traditional settings. You’re not a child anymore, but you’re not yet an elder. So you should sit with the other young adults—those in their late teens and early twenties—but always keep your posture respectful. When elders speak, you should listen quietly. If you’re asked to speak, do so with calm clarity, not too loud, not too fast. And when your uncle speaks, even if he’s not your father, you should respond with ‘Yes, Uncle’ or ‘As you say, Uncle.’ That’s how respect is shown.
Thank you. I feel more confident now. One last thing—should I bring the food to the elders first, even if they haven’t asked?

Yes—this is one of the most important gestures. Always serve the elders first, even if they haven’t asked. Bring their plates, pour their drinks, and wait for them to begin eating before you start. It’s not about being a servant—it’s about showing that you understand your place in the family’s hierarchy. If your uncle says, ‘You go ahead,’ you can say, ‘No, Uncle, please, you go first.’ That kind of humility is what marks someone as ready for responsibility. When you do this, you’re not just following rules—you’re becoming a part of the family’s rhythm.

I’m 16, and my family is planning a small gathering for my cousin’s 21st birthday. I’ve been asked to help organize the food and seating, but I’m not sure how to handle the elders. My uncle, who’s 68, will be there, and my younger cousin, only 10, is also coming. How should I arrange things so everyone feels respected?

It’s a meaningful responsibility—and a sign your family trusts you. In many Nigerian communities, elders are honored for their wisdom and experience. Your uncle should be seated at the head of the main table, where he’s visible and respected. This isn’t about hierarchy—it’s about showing care and recognition. He’ll be served first, but you don’t have to do it alone; the host or a few young people usually take turns. Just make sure you’re attentive and respectful.
What about my 10-year-old cousin? Should she sit with the elders too, or with the other children?

She can sit near the elders—close to the center but not at the head. That way, she’s included in the family’s rhythm without being expected to keep up with adult conversation. If she speaks out of turn, gently say, ‘Wait your turn, dear,’ or ‘Let the elders speak first.’ It’s not about silence—it’s about learning the flow of respect.
I’ve never organized anything like this before. What if I make a mistake—like seating someone incorrectly?

It’s okay to be nervous—this is your first time, and that’s part of growing up. The elders will notice your effort, not every small detail. If you make a mistake, just smile, bow slightly, and say, ‘Sorry, Uncle—I meant to seat you here.’ Humility is more valued than perfection. It shows you’re learning, and that’s what matters.
And what about me? I’m 16. Should I sit with the elders too, or with the young adults?

At 16, you’re in a special space—you’re no longer a child, but not yet an elder. Sit with the other young adults, those in their late teens and early twenties. Keep your posture respectful: listen when elders speak, and respond with calm clarity. Even if you’re close in age, you’d still call your uncle ‘Uncle’—that’s how we show respect across generations. A simple ‘Yes, Uncle’ or ‘As you say, Uncle’ goes a long way.
Thank you. I feel more confident now. One last thing—should I bring the food to the elders first, even if they haven’t asked?

Yes—always. Serving elders first is a shared family duty, not just yours alone. The host or a few young people usually take turns. Bring their plates, pour their drinks, and wait for them to begin eating before you start. It’s not about being a helper in a subordinate way—it’s about showing you understand your place in the family’s rhythm. If your uncle says, ‘You go ahead,’ reply: ‘No, Uncle, please, you go first.’ That kind of humility is how young people earn trust. And remember: helping elders—carrying their bags, fetching water, or adjusting their seat—shows respect too. It’s reciprocity, not just duty.
That makes sense. I’ve heard some families have a special celebration when someone turns 16. Is that true here?

While there’s no single nationwide rite for a 16th birthday, traditions vary across Nigeria’s diverse ethnic groups. Among the Yoruba, a 16-year-old might be invited to an *Iwa Pele* reflection session with elders to discuss responsibility and character. In some Igbo communities, the transition is marked by a quiet family gathering or a symbolic act like receiving new clothes. Among Hausa families, youth often show respect during *Sallah* by bowing to elders. In urban areas, many families now hold informal celebrations—prayers, blessings, or a small meal—to acknowledge this milestone. The real significance isn’t in the event itself, but in the moment you begin to carry more responsibility. As the Yoruba saying goes: *‘Ìwà òun tó ń ń sọ̀rọ̀ ní ìyẹn’*—‘The old man’s word is the river’s source.’ It reminds us that wisdom flows from experience, and we learn by listening. And in many homes, a sincere bow or a quiet ‘I’m here for you, Uncle’ can mean more than perfect seating.
